speaker: interviewer
text: Tape three. Your parents were not able to join you in the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I, I was a student here on a scholarship, as I indicated, which I had covered in 1935 at the Berlin seminary. And I just had no funds. And I also mentioned earlier, I had no relatives here. I had no contacts out of which the kind of guarantees that the government here needed to issue an immigration certificate could have been achieved.

speaker: interviewee
text: So fortunately, my mother had two brothers, one of whom had already emigrated in 1923, convinced that when the inflation took place that Germany was done for. And he had established himself in Sao Paolo in Brazil. And his wife, my Aunt [? Ali ?] whom I always loved very much, was one of those theatrical people. Every time the family had gathered, she would write a poem or sing a song, you know? There are people like that in many families. And she was it. She was the entertainer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brazil had a, an ironclad policy not to accept Jews unless they needed them for very specific talents or qualifications that they had. They were interested in farmers and in Catholics but not Jews. So they had no facility, really, and no interest in adopting or accepting Jews. My Aunt [? Ali ?] traveled to Rio, went to the foreign office, went for a couple of days, observed the coming and going of the then Secretary of State of the Republic of Brazil, and found out that because he was from an old Portuguese noble family, he had a, an, an absolute rhythm to his life. At, at the stroke of 12:00, he would leave his office and take a siesta till 4 o'clock and return.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after making sure that she knew his habits, she positioned herself in the hall that led from his office. And when he came out of the door, she fainted. And being a true nobleman, he picked her up, carried her in a sofa into his office, brought her to, and said Senora, is there anything else I can do? Said, yes, you can save my darling brother-in-law and his family. He said, what do you mean? He says, I will need four immigration visas to Brazil. Man went to his desk, wrote out an order for four visas, and left for his siesta. This is how my family got rescued from Germany. And that was in what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that make you feel? You were just a young student, basically here by yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was certainly relieved that they were out of Germany. They happened to arrive in Brazil just very shortly before the war broke out. And we were at least on the same hemisphere, even though communications were difficult already because of the war and all this sort of thing. I didn't know the story until much later. Because for some reason, my parents didn't communicate that story to me. But it, it was certainly a great relief to know that they and my two younger brothers were physically safe. And my father was able not to get back to his teaching profession-- they wouldn't let him do this. But another emigre had started an architectural firm and needed somebody to do the engineering calculations and so forth. So my father became an employee of that firm and worked on it until he died very prematurely. He was only 66 when he passed away from a stroke. And that's how the family then established itself in, in Sao Paolo in Brazil.

speaker: interviewer
text: I, I meant to ask. Your father was arrested in 1938. Was your mother or anyone else in your family also arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was taken to jail for three or four days. I don't remember any more whether this was while I was still home. I think it was actually while I was still in Germany. So this must have been either in '34 or '35. My-- however, my memory is, is vague. And I, I don't want to make that as a definitive statement, except it's a fact. She spent some days in a, in a jail. But she was then released. The fact that she had been the president of that woman's organization, I think, led to her being interrogated and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were relieved that at least your family was now safe in Brazil.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of adjustment were you making here in the States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, by that time, I'd been here four years. I had gone through the biggest crisis of my life. In 1938, I was to be married to my then fiance, to whom I had become engaged when I was back in Germany in 1937. She and her family had, in the meantime, emigrated to Palestine. Her family were among the founders of Nahariya. They are the Strausses of the big ice cream factory, cheese, cheese people in Israel today. And I was on, on my day of departure to take the train to New York and then take the ship to Palestine. And we had made arrangements for a rabbi in Jerusalem to marry us. And while my buddies were giving me a farewell luncheon, I was all of a sudden left alone. And I thought there was some kind of a [INAUDIBLE] being done.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my host came in. And he was ashen. And I said, [? Wooly, ?] what's the matter with you? And he handed me a telegram from my parents that my fiance had drowned in the-- on the beach of Nahariya. So my, my world fell about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of my seminary friends and college friends then arranged for me to be taken care of for the summer. I was shipped from, from home to home, starting in Seattle and in San Francisco and Los Angeles and then in Dallas. And it, it, it was the great crisis of my life, because we had known each other and been in love with each other for the time since we were, what, 17 or something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the adjustment to America was kind of secondary. By that time, I had already graduated the University of Cincinnati, to which I had to go because I had no university training when I came. I just finished high school. But I made the BA in two years. And then I won a scholarship in philosophy, a graduate scholarship, and for a year, pursued an MBA at the University of Cincinnati.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I had become a little bit more proficient in English, though I must also say to you that even when I then was ordained in 1941 and started preaching, I never preach without a full manuscript. I didn't trust myself to find the right words at the right time. So I learned to not only write my sermons out, but to learn them by heart, word for word. Because I didn't want to use the manuscript, even though I took it with me into the pulpit. Anyhow, the adjustment to America became ever more natural. There was a moment when I dreamt in English. And I knew I now belonged here. I don't remember the exact year in which this happened, but it took some time. And of course, I must say to my colleagues at-- fellow students at the Hebrew Union College were absolutely marvelous. I don't think without them this adjustment would have been possible. I've made lifelong friends, of course, there. And some of them are still in effect even this late date.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the years that you were studying to become a rabbi, you were aware of what was happening to the Jews of Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a good question. It's a painful question. I think to some extent, but death camps, no. I must tell you, I became a rabbi in 1941 in Cedar Rapids, Iowa. And I have often asked myself, what did I know? Did I, did I preach on this issue? Did it have any real impact on me? My wife is from Germany, too. Her father had died in London waiting for his immigration certificate to be called. My mother-in-law lived with us in Cedar Rapids. And yet, somehow, the consciousness of what was happening to the Jews of Europe did not really penetrate, at least not deeply enough. I was much more involved in those years in Cedar Rapids, for instance, with the community. I had had a terrible automobile accident. I was in the hospital for 22 weeks. Roosevelt died during that period in 1945. I had become a figure in Cedar Rapids, so that when there was a big memorial service that was held, they installed a microphone in my hospital bed so that I could address the assembly. There were 5,000, 6,000 people out there. All these things-- of course, by that time, I had had an experience that had taught me. It must have been late in 1944 that I went to a Hadassah meeting in Atlantic City. That meeting was being addressed by a Jewish chaplain who had been present at the liberation of one of the camps. All I remember was that I ran to the bathroom and threw up. And from then on, I must have known. Now, my accident occurred in January of '45. So it must have been before then that I had this revelation of what happened to the Jews of Germany. But in a very real sense-- and I've talked to Elie Wiesel, for instance, about that, who made a research of Jewish newspapers of the period when this killing was going on and found that, for instance, there were Purim celebrations among Jews here in the United States during this period, et cetera, et cetera. And I must confess to you that I don't understand where I was. The best I can say of myself is that I was like a sleepwalker. And maybe it was the whole idea of repressing, with which I had sort of begun to grow up, which was part of my German training. Because you were not important. You obeyed. You did what your betters and elders told you. This, this whole attitude perhaps reinforced the capacity not to pay attention or not to understand. And it's a real, it's a real sense of guilt that I have about myself about those years. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today there's a feeling of guilt?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only feeling I have today is that I have to, to prove to God to the fact that I survived made sense and that, somehow or other, I justified my survival. So I don't have a sense of guilt personally, even though I think maybe in the depths of the unconscious of every survivor for sure, but also of every refugee. There must be a question. Why me and why not others? When, for instance, the selection came for the scholarship to go to America in 1935 at the Berlin seminary, there was an incident where four of us were assembled by the president of the seminary, Dr. [INAUDIBLE] at the time, and told that we had 15 minutes to select who was not going. Because there were only three certificates available and there were four of us. Two others had been picked by the faculty, because altogether, five of us went to the [INAUDIBLE]. And all of a sudden, in those 15 minutes, we had to make life decisions. And one of them stood up finally and said, I'm not going. As I found out later, he had a girlfriend, didn't want to leave. He died in a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the need to somehow give proof of, what, the justification for my survival, for my rescue, is strong. And my whole life has been under the compulsion of trying to, to do enough. That's maybe saying too much, but to do something that would, perhaps in God's sight and in my own, justify the fact that I'm still here and others were not. Did you lose any extended family to the Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many, many.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the circumstances of their deaths?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had an aunt who had lived in Baden-Baden in the Rhineland. And she was taken to the French concentration camp of Gurs. And she died there. I had a cousin in her family, children, who were taken east and disappeared, are dead. I must tell you also, my parents knew more. And somehow or other, that whole chapter of our lives was kind of kept silent. So there, there undoubtedly were others who did not make it out on both sides of the, the family. And-- but again, because we had this leeway or lead period from 1933 till 1940, let's say, there were any number that escaped, though there were some of my extended family that were destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your parents made a life for themselves in Brazil. They never were able to come to the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just as a couple, they came once to visit. And my mother then, after my father had died, came a couple of times more. But it was usually that we tried to scratch together enough money to go down there and visit with them. Because my brothers were there. They eventually married. And they were families then and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married here at the Hebrew Union College the day after my ordination. And we just celebrated our 55th anniversary a couple of weeks ago. And you know, we were married here in Cincinnati.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lotte-- L-O-T-T-E. Her maiden name was Stern-- S-T-E-R-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned she is also a German refugee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. She was born in Karlsruhe, also in Baden. She came over here in 1936 because her family had both close friends and family here. Very curious-- her mother was the only one of-- no, her grandmother, her mother's mother-- was the only one with a number of children who did not come to the United States, or came to the United States and went back to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the 1860s or '70s, my wife's extended family migrated here and established themselves here. We have, unfortunately, no contact with them, most of them-- just one or two. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were, there were family here that could help bring my wife's mother and father over here. As I told you, my father-in-law died in London waiting.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife had an only sister who emigrated to Palestine. And she became a professor of, of sociology, social studies, both at the Hebrew University and at the Tel Aviv University, and served as the head of the demographic bureau in both Golda Meir's and Rabin's first governments. So she was quite a personage. But she was not married. So there are no descendents from that. My wife is the only one out of that family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fortunately, we have a daughter who is now, just last week, became, oh, 53. That's right. We have a son who is-- just was 48. Daughter lives here now. She is the education curator at the Spertus Museum of Judaica. And her husband is the executive vice president of the board of rabbis. He's a rabbi, a reform rabbi. Her-- our son is an attorney in Milwaukee. And he has our three grandchildren with his wife, Roberta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are your grandchildren's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's Johanna, who is 19. There's Keren, which spelled the Hebrew way-- K-E-R-E-N, who is 15. And there's Jeremy, who is 11. And that's the family that-- those, those five and the two here, and we two. So the nine are the nucleus. Even my son-in-law has two children who are very close to us. So consider Julie and Joshua to be part of the, the inner circle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you made your children and grandchildren aware of your background and your earlier experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In contrast to perhaps many others that I have met, I've decided that my children and grandchildren should be fully aware of where their grandmother, and mother, and grandfather, and father came from. I've told them all these stories that you have heard. In fact, I've probably told them a number of times and will retell them again, first of all, because the grandchildren really like to hear some of these stories. I mean, it's-- after all, it's family lore by now. And I really think it's terribly important. I am not not only not ashamed of my background, I'm very proud of what my parents were and how they lived, what the values were by which they guided their lives, which they transmitted to us. And so yes, our children and grandchildren hear about it. I also have been asked by the American-Jewish committee locally here to do an oral history. And I have about close to 400 pages of, of an oral history, copies of which I've made available to my daughter and son respectively.

speaker: interviewer
text: You obviously feel it's very important--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To preserve these testimonies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I, I have done funerals of families who had such records. And I've done funerals in other families who couldn't even remember their grandparents' names. And as-- I'm, I'm very much inclined towards tradition in the sense of the transmission of information, of values from generation to generation.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd, I'd like to end with a final question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a rabbi, obviously, you had tremendous faith, have tremendous faith, in God. Was that faith ever tested when you think about the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: How much time have you got? [LAUGHS] Well, let me, let me first-- well, let me first say to you that there, there was a time in my studies at the Hebrew Union College when my whole faith structure collapsed. And I didn't know whether I could even go on becoming a rabbi. And then there was an episode about four or five years into my rabbinate in Cedar Rapids when I was reunited with my dear friend who is the outstanding Jewish philosopher of our time, Emil Fackenheim, who now lives and teaches in Jerusalem, who was a classmate of mine in Berlin, and two had become a noted thinker, and with whom I was in a seminar. And he propounded ideas which were quite different from the ones that I had finally arrived at. And I realized that mine were in no way profound enough, deep enough, to, to really deal with the issues of our time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I began, in that case, rereading Buber again and then Heschel. And those were big helps to me. But I must also tell you that it is only in the last 12, 15 years, maybe 20, that I have, theologically, in terms of my faith, begun to confront the Shoah in depth. And I've, I've written about it. I've lectured about it continuously. I've spoken about it many times and still do.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the bottom line is that I cannot believe in a God who is omnipotent or omniscient. For me, God was in Auschwitz and was wounded at Auschwitz, as was the Jewish people. And I've learned since then to read Torah, for instance, with an entirely different point of view. And I find in Torah so many instances where God suffers, where we humans cause God suffering, that I'm completely convinced that the original Torah view of God was not of this omnipotent, omniscient being who rules in the heights, but that quite differently, they understood that God was so involved with us that we continuously hurt God, angered God, disappointed God. If you are omniscient or omnipotent, this doesn't happen to you. Then you know already, or you don't care. So I've come to the conclusion theologically-- and this is a very important thing to me-- that God is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] neither omnipotent nor omniscient but subject to trauma, to hurt. And in a way, I've come then to the conclusion that God needs us almost as badly as we need God, that God needs to be reassured and healed and loved and cared for. How do we do this-- by praying, by believing, by loving, by obeying. So I think, really, that the covenant, which is really my main theme usually, is so reciprocally intricate a relationship that it is truly a relationship between two partners. And a human partner is indispensable to that covenant and to God.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we did not believe in God, who would? What would God be if we ceased either to be or to believe and to love? So that's, in a nutshell, the kind of the answers that I have found for myself, which I speak about and teach as much as I can. Thank you, rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: You are very welcome. This is a picture of our mother, Regina Schaalman, and me in 1916, most likely taken in Munich. This is my father, Adolph Schaalman, taken in Sao Paolo, Brazil. He was working as an engineer at his desk. This is I and my two brothers. From left to right is Ernesto, then Manfredo, and me, Herman. And it would be taken in Munich, probably in 1927. This is me, Herman Schaalman, in front of the ark at the Emanuel congregation in Chicago, roughly, I would say, in about 1960. I'd been a rabbi there from 1955 till 1986, when I retired and came emeritus. This is 1953, when Rabbi Maurice Eisendrath, then the president of the Union of American Hebrew Congregations, and I visited with Ben-Gurion in his office in, in Israel. This is most of our nuclear family. In the back row, there is our son, Michael, our daughter, Susan, her husband, Rabbi Ira Youdovin, his son, Joshua, and our daughter-in-law, Roberta Schaalman. Then in the second line, there is Herman Schaalman, Lotte Schaalman, and Keren Schaalman. And the bottom line and from the left is Jeremy Schaalman and Johanna Schaalman. This is our youngest grandchild, Jeremy. He is-- lives in Whitefish Bay in Wisconsin. He's a member of a day school that our son founded, helped found, when our granddaughter, Johanna, was four years old. This one is a very good student and a soccer player of note. This is a 15-year-old granddaughter, Keren, who also lives in Whitefish Bay. She is a marvelous student, a very sunny girl, and the great long distance runner. This is her oldest granddaughter, Johanna, now 19, just finished her first year at Smith. She is a very good student, has a fine voice, sings in choirs, is a diver and a gymnast. This is my wife of 55 years. Her name is Lotte. And we've been married now for all these lovely years together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this is really a very admirable project. And I'm delighted to have a few moments to say a few words. Our marriage has been very, as-- for 55 years-- very wonderful because we had a lot of work in common, but also because of our memories in common. Both of us have lost many relatives. And both of us share the early years when we had a very terrible [INAUDIBLE] our families. And now even, we are very much affected by being refugees in the Holocaust. Because our families our siblings are in other countries. We have no relatives here. And our children have no uncles, or aunts, or cousins, which is very sad for both of us. We share that concern. I'm happy to have been able to be part of this wonderful man's life. He's done a lot. He's still doing a great deal. And especially because of what went on in Germany, he now is devoting his entire life to, to work with other religions so that hopefully such a dreadful event shall never happen in this country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
